source.title: The salience network is responsible for switching between the default mode network and the central executive network: replication from DCM

confidence_rationale: Independent methodological replication of Sridharan 2008's central result. Uses dynamic causal modelling (DCM) on ICA-extracted network time courses in resting state, applied to two independent datasets. Confirms that the salience network drives switching between DMN and CEN. Moderate-high source quality (NeuroImage; rigorous Bayesian model comparison). Replication strengthens the architectural license for SD-032a's coordinator role. Confidence not higher because the study is resting-state only (no task manipulation) and the network labels inherit Sridharan's two-network frame -- same mapping limitations apply.
failure_signatures: If ree-v3 substrates reproduce coordinated downstream gate changes without requiring a salience-aggregate coordinator input, the DCM replication's architectural claim is violated.

If the operating_mode vector in SD-032a can be stably driven by a single subdivision's output (e.g. dACC alone) rather than a weighted aggregate, the 'salience drives switching' finding is not respected.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Using DCM on ICA-derived time courses in two independent resting-state datasets, the salience network (AIC + dACC) is shown to drive bidirectional switching between the default mode network and the central executive network.
mapping.ree_translation: Replication evidence that the biological coordinator role of the salience network is reproducible with a different causal-inference method than Granger. In ree-v3, the SalienceCoordinator's aggregating architecture (reading AIC / dACC / PCC / pACC analogues and emitting operating_mode) is the computational counterpart.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Resting-state only. The study does not test whether salience-driven switching generalises across different task demands or operating regimes. Also, DCM's inference is Bayesian model comparison over candidate connectivity structures, not a direct test of the 'coordinator' computation; it shows which network drives switches, not what aggregation rule the driver implements. ree-v3's softmax-over-affinity-logits is therefore architectural convention, not something Goulden constrains.
mapping.source_context: Healthy adult humans; 3T fMRI; resting state; two independent datasets; DCM on ICA-extracted canonical networks (DMN, CEN, SN).

confidence_components.notes: Solid methodological replication raises aggregate confidence that Sridharan's architectural claim is robust, not artefactual. Mapping fidelity is similar to Sridharan's: the switching role transfers, the aggregation rule does not. Transfer risk slightly higher because resting-state-only results may not generalise to task-engaged regimes where ree-v3 operates most of the time.
